title: Integer types
instruction:
C# has several integer sizes. `int` (32-bit) is the default.

Run this.
successMessage: Each integer type has a range. _ is a digit separator (ignored by compiler). L suffix marks a long literal.

title: Floating-point types
instruction:
`double` (64-bit) is the default. `float` (32-bit) needs an `f` suffix. `decimal` is for exact decimal arithmetic.

Run this.
successMessage: 0.1m + 0.2m = 0.3 exactly. 0.1 + 0.2 in double would give 0.30000000000000004. Use decimal for money!

title: Boolean type
instruction:
`bool` is `true` or `false`. Comparison and logical operators produce booleans.

Run this.
successMessage: &&, ||, ! are the logical operators. C# booleans print as True/False (capitalised).

title: char type
instruction:
`char` stores a single Unicode character in single quotes.

Run this.
successMessage: (int)letter casts the char to its Unicode code point. 'A' is 65. Casting is explicit in C#.

title: Explicit casting
instruction:
C# requires explicit casts for narrowing conversions (e.g., double to int).

Run this.
successMessage: (int) truncates (does not round). 1000 % 256 = 232 — byte wraps. Explicit casts make potential data loss visible.

title: Implicit conversions
instruction:
Widening conversions (small -> large) are automatic.

Run this.
successMessage: int fits in long and double — no data loss possible, so the conversion is automatic. Narrowing (double->int) requires an explicit cast.

title: Convert class
instruction:
`Convert` provides safe conversions with clear naming.

Run this.
successMessage: Convert.ToInt32 is safer than a cast — it handles string -> int, bool -> int, etc. For string parsing, int.Parse and int.TryParse are also common.

title: TryParse for safe input
instruction:
`TryParse` returns false instead of throwing an exception on invalid input.

Run this.
successMessage: TryParse uses the 'out' parameter pattern to return both success/failure and the parsed value. No exception on failure.
